entry.title: Body of missing man recovered from Payette River
entry.summary: A Boise County Sheriff’s office vehicle is pictured in this undated photo. BOISE COUNTY — Search and rescue crews have recovered the body of a man who’s been missing for several weeks. Officials from multiple agencies found and recovered the body of 67-year-old John Barmettler from the Payette River on Wednesday, June 17, the Boise County Sheriff’s Office posted on social media. The recovery ended a search-and-rescue effort that has been ongoing since June 1. Kayakers spotted Barmettler’s abandoned vehicle down a steep embankment near the South Fork of the Payette River on June 1, and it was recovered the next day. In the time since, the search for Barmettler has included crews from several agencies, including the Boise County Sheriff’s Office, Garden Valley Fire & EMS, Boise County Search & Rescue, Bear Valley Rafting, Cascade Raft & Kayak, Idaho Mountain Search & Rescue, Mountain West Commercial Diving, Gem County Towing, Garden Valley Market, “and many community members and volunteers throughout the Garden Valley community,” the sheriff’s office said. “Our thoughts and prayers are with Barmettler’s family and all those whose lives he touched and positively impacted,” the sheriff’s office said on social media. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. entry.title: Off-duty law enforcement officers, family member killed in Idaho crash
entry.summary: First responders work the scene of a crash on U.S. Highway 12 near Kooskia where three motorcyclists were killed on June 16, 2026. KOOSKIA — Two of the three people killed in a crash Tuesday on an Idaho highway have been identified as Colorado law enforcement officers. The Idaho County Coroner’s Office on Wednesday evening identified the victims of the crash as Jeremy Coleman, 45, of Berthoud, Colorado; Ethan Powers, 35 of Timnath, Colorado; and Nathan McCormick, 26, of Loveland, Colorado. The Colorado Fraternal Order of Police said on social media that Coleman and Powers were a sergeant and deputy, respectively, in the Larimer County Sheriff’s Office, and that McCormick was Coleman’s son-in-law. The Colorado FOP said the law enforcement officers were off duty at the time of the crash. The Larimer County Sheriff’s Office said on social media that the three were vacationing together on a motorcycle trip. “Sgt. Coleman and Deputy Powers dedicated their lives to protecting their community, and their impact will continue to be felt by those who had the privilege of serving alongside them,” the Colorado FOP’s post reads. Neale Brewer, a 60-year-old woman from Colorado Springs, Colorado, has been arrested and charged with three counts of felony vehicular manslaughter, according to Idaho State Police, the Idaho County Jail and online court records. She is being held on a $1 million bond. ISP stated that Brewer was driving a 2019 Ford F-150 westbound on U.S. Highway 12, near Kooskia, at about 4 p.m. Tuesday, June 16, when she allegedly attempted to pass another vehicle in a no-passing zone. The pickup crossed the double yellow center line and collided with three motorcycles traveling eastbound. All three motorcyclists died at the scene. Brewer was transported to a local hospital for medical evaluation and was arrested following her release. “There are no good words in a situation like this,” the Larimer County Sheriff’s Office said on social media. “Put simply, these were incredible people who dedicated their lives to making Larimer County better every single day. They trained hard, they served well, and they loved life. As we walk the long and winding path of grief alongside their families, our resolve is stronger than ever.” Larimer County is located in northern Colorado and encompasses cities that include Fort Collins and Loveland. Kooskia is a small town in north-central Idaho, located about 75 miles east of Lewiston. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. A Colorado woman faces vehicular manslaughter charges after she allegedly killed three motorcyclists while passing in a no-passing zone. entry.title: Gov. Little says 'Idaho is prepared' as drought, low snowpack mark start of fire season
entry.summary: Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. Nampa Fire Protection District Chief Kirk Carpenter, president of the Idaho Fire Chiefs Association, adds to a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. BOISE — Firefighting agencies gathered Tuesday at the Idaho State Capitol for a pre-season wildfire briefing. As fire season enters into its early stages, state leaders underscored that the historically hot and dry conditions bring the potential for a significant and costly fire season. Officials who spoke at Tuesday’s Land Board meeting and subsequent pre-fire season press conference consistently said widespread drought conditions are expected to continue, conducive to a heightened fire season. As of Tuesday — still very early in the fire season — there have been 59 fires across 549 acres, 26 of which were human caused, 13 were natural and 20 have a cause yet to be determined, the Idaho Department of Lands’ (IDL’s) division administrator of operations Jake Strohmeyer said. Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. While the winter months saw near-normal precipitation, warm temperatures resulted in above-normal snowpack only being observed at elevations above 6,000 feet near the panhandle and 7,000 feet in southern Idaho. Jim Wallmann, a Bureau of Land Management meteorologist at the National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC), said these temperatures were the second warmest on record, averaging 6 degrees above normal from December through May. The limited snowpack in Idaho, which was at a record low as of April 1, has impacted the fire fuels in the state, with conditions characterized as “above normal” for much of southern Idaho. After a wet April in southwest Idaho, an abundance of fine fuels, such as tall grasses, have grown in the region, Wallmann said. If the dry and hot conditions continue as expected, these fuels only add to the potential for future wildfires. In some respects, Wallmann said what Idaho is observing is not unique. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, about 60% of the country is experiencing drought conditions. Though these conditions are starting to show signs of improvement in the southeastern U.S., the snow drought in the western U.S. is contributing to worsening drought conditions. The most recent national seasonal drought forecast indicated that drought will persist through much of Idaho at least through to the end of August. In April, the Idaho Department of Water Resources issued an emergency drought declaration for each of the state’s 44 counties. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, Owyhee and Twin Falls counties are experiencing “exceptional drought,” the highest intensity on the drought scale. Though conditions conducive to wildfire are expected to persist over the coming months, officials reiterated IDL was in a positive staffing position before fire season gets into full swing. Across IDL’s 50-engine fleet, Strohmeyer said 36 will be fully staffed, 11 more than the previous fire season. In terms of individual personnel, the department has 260 operational firefighters heading into the fire season, 31 more than last year. He added that 48% of these firefighters have one or more years of fire experience and all of the department’s full-time firefighting positions have been filled. The department is also bringing back a Type 1 helicopter based out of Coeur d’Alene which just came under contract, but is still working to bolster its air resources for the future. While IDL works to restart its helitack program, which provides aerial support for wildfires, the department will be partnering with the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources to bring on their Type 3 helicopter along with their crew. Strohmeyer said this arrangement will allow firefighters to receive needed training for when IDL’s helitack program restarts, potentially as soon as next year. Idaho Department of Lands Director Dustin Miller adds to a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. IDL Director Dustin Miller further highlighted 11 IDL wildfire detection cameras were operating across the state, with eight additional cameras due to be installed this year. This comes in addition to the official adoption of “Ready, Set, Go!” as the state’s evacuation messaging framework to ensure consistent standards are in place when communicating wildfire response. “If we aren’t the first, we’re one of the first states in the United States to actually have a statewide mass evacuation protocol,” Nampa Fire Protection District Chief and President of the Idaho Fire Chiefs Association Kirk Carpenter said, “and we can only do that when we have relationships that span across to all state and federal partnerships.”

entry.title: Federal judge blocks parts of Idaho bathroom bill from taking effect
entry.summary: Idaho lawmakers passed a sweeping bathroom ban March 27, 2026, approving legislation that would make it a crime for transgender people to use the bathroom that matches their gender identity, even inside privately owned businesses. The bill that would have criminalized transgender people for using public restrooms consistent with their gender identity will not fully go into effect July 1, after a federal judge granted injunctive relief Tuesday to Idahoans who sued to block the implementation of that aspect of the law. House Bill 752, which Gov. Brad Little signed earlier this spring, labeled the use of bathrooms or changing rooms “designated for use by the opposite biological sex” as a misdemeanor for the first instance and a felony punishable by up to five years’ imprisonment for the second. The American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) and its Idaho chapter, as well as Lambda Legal, represent six transgender Idaho plaintiffs in the case known as Jackson-Edney v. Labrador. The plaintiffs are not challenging the changing room aspect of the legislation, and the injunction blocks the ban from taking effect with regards to single-user public restrooms. “This ruling means trans folks in Idaho can continue participating in public life without the threat of being arrested for using the bathroom,” the ACLU of Idaho’s legal director, Paul Carlos Southwick, said in a statement. “This ruling will relieve that anxiety for our trans friends and neighbors.” Vagueness was one ground on which enforcement of the statute was blocked. “The Court finds that H.B. 752 is unconstitutionally vague because it fails to provide sufficient standards for law enforcement,” Judge Amanda Brailsford’s court order read. For example, the terms “dire need” and “reasonably available” are used in reference to circumstances in which exceptions can be made to the ban, but the legislation’s text does not give definitions for either term. The definition of an individual’s biological sex — whether it is determined by an officer’s estimation of one’s visual appearance or by DNA collection that cannot be mandated without a warrant — was another issue the ruling cast as vague and arbitrary. “Without statutory definitions, narrowing context, or settled legal meanings H.B. 752 invites arbitrary and discriminatory enforcement,” the order reads. “Having concluded that Plaintiffs are likely to succeed on the merits of their vagueness claim, the Court finds irreparable harm will likely occur in the absence of preliminary relief.” The attorney general’s office had argued in favor of HB 752 on the basis of “privacy and safety concerns that have always motivated sex-segregated bathrooms,” as Solicitor General Michael Zarian told the court June 5. Brailsford said that Idaho already has laws addressing misconduct in restrooms, such as those against sexual battery, malicious harassment and indecent exposure. In a Tuesday statement, Attorney General Raúl Labrador said that the ruling was a misinterpretation of HB 752’s intent and the state’s position. “Biological sex is not vague, and neither is the law,” Labrador said. “The good news is that the ruling is narrow. Idaho’s law remains enforceable in most settings, including changing rooms and many restrooms.” The law may still be enforced with respect to multiple-stall restrooms if no single-stall facility is available. Labrador expressed his intention to appeal the decision, which remains in place as the court continues to weigh HB 752’s constitutionality. Six transgender plaintiffs are suing to prevent Idaho''s new bathroom law from being enacted July 1. They are being represented by the ACLU. entry.title: David W. Hahn named lone finalist to become Boise State University's next president
entry.summary: David W. Hahn, lone finalist to be the eighth president at Boise State University. BOISE — After a yearlong search, Boise State University has found its new president. The Idaho State Board of Education on Tuesday announced David W. Hahn as the lone finalist to serve as the eighth president of Boise State University. Hahn will succeed Marlene Tromp, who in March 2025 announced she was leaving Boise to become president of the University of Vermont. Jeremiah Shinn, Boise State’s vice president for student affairs and enrollment management, served as interim president, though in April was tabbed by the University of Montana to be its next president. Nancy Glenn, vice president of research and economic development, stepped in as acting president while the search at Boise State continued. Hahn currently serves as the Craig M. Berge Dean of the College of Engineering at the University of Arizona. “After a rigorous and thorough national search, I am confident the State Board has identified the right leader for this moment at Boise State,” David Turnbull, chairman of the Presidential Search Committee and member of the Idaho State Board of Education, said in a press statement. “Dr. Hahn brings exceptional credentials as someone who has consistently built institutions that are stronger and more innovative than when he arrived. Boise State and the state of Idaho are well-served by this selection.” Hahn brings more than 30 years of combined experience in engineering education, research and administration. “Boise State University has exceptional momentum, and my pledge is to continue guiding our contributions in all aspects, from a strong commitment to delivering a high-value and affordable education to our students, innovating in support of state, regional and national research needs, partnering with the communities and industries of Idaho, fostering the winning culture of Bronco Athletics, while successfully navigating the changing landscape of higher education,” Hahn said in a press statement. “This all starts with people and throughout my interactions, I have seen a group fully dedicated to the success of Boise State University and I am honored to join the team.” In accordance with Idaho state law, Hahn was named as the sole finalist for the position, officially beginning a 10-business-day period prior to a formal vote by the Idaho State Board of Education. You can email the Idaho State Board of Education with your feedback at . Trevor Hawes is the executive editor of the Idaho Press. BOISE — Ten days remain in the countdown to vote on Boise State University’s next president, with David W. entry.title: Gov. Little announces new working group for university athletics programs
entry.summary: Members of the Boise State football team run a drill this spring during a practice at Albertsons Stadium. BOISE — Gov. Brad Little announced the formation of a new College Athletics Working Group on Monday, with a stated aim of keeping Idaho universities’ athletics programs competitive. The group will be hearing from each Idaho public university’s athletic department over the coming months to identify both “policy solutions and investment opportunities tailored to each institution’s needs,” a news release from Little’s office said. “Idaho’s colleges and universities deliver real value to students, communities, our economy, and our athletics programs are a meaningful part of that. This working group will make sure we’re not sitting on the sidelines,” Little said in the release. The working group is set to be chaired by Cortney Liddiard, an Idaho Falls businessman and former CEO of commercial real estate firm Ball Ventures. The remaining board members, comprised of educational, business and legislative leadership, is as follows: The working group arrives as the White House and Congress are exploring federal reforms to college sports. While the release notes these efforts are pending, the working group will focus on options at the state and local levels that can serve to benefit public universities. The group is expected to present its findings to Little in September along with recommendations for how to best support the “long-term competitiveness and sustainability of intercollegiate athletics in Idaho,” the release said. The working group arrives several years after the NCAA instituted a Name, Image and Likeness (NIL) policy in 2021 to permit student-athletes to make money off of their personal brand after previously being forbidden from doing so. Today, these student-athlete deals still lack federal regulation and have instead been met with a patchwork of state regulations that lack consistency in how NIL arrangements are allowed to operate. While the majority of states have since enacted respective state legislation, Idaho is one of more than a dozen without statewide rules. In a show of support for federal NIL regulation, the Idaho Legislature approved Senate Joint Memorial 114 this past legislative session, urging Congress to take “action to prevent program cuts, escalating costs, and competitive disparities.” While Idaho’s joint memorial was being debated, representatives for Boise State offered support for national regulation of these deals, contending that the current arrangement has resulted in colleges in other states playing by different rules. Currently, states that go without regulation of NIL deals operate under rules adopted by an individual university and the NCAA’s overarching regulatory framework. That framework shifted significantly last year, after a federal lawsuit was settled to allow schools to directly share revenue with student-athletes, among other changes, as was reported by the Associated Press. “We need a single uniform national framework for NIL, revenue sharing and eligibility status to preempt state laws and litigation,” Boise State athletic director Jeramiah Dickey said in prior testimony on the legislature’s memorial. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. entry.title: Latest CDC data puts Idaho town among nation's top COVID wastewater hot spots
entry.summary: Signs lead people to a COVID-19 vaccination clinic setup at a park in Caldwell. The COVID-19 pandemic may feel like a distant memory for most Pocatello residents, but something unusual is showing up in the city’s wastewater — and public health officials are paying attention. According to data released earlier this month by the CDC’s National Wastewater Surveillance System, Bannock County is currently listed as having very high levels of COVID-19 in its wastewater — the highest category on the CDC’s five-tier scale. It’s worth noting that the CDC’s designation for Bannock County is based entirely on testing conducted in Pocatello, which is the only city in the county participating in the national surveillance program. No other communities in Bannock County are being tested. Whether the rest of the county mirrors what Pocatello’s wastewater is showing is unknown — though given that Pocatello is by far the county’s largest city and its regional hub, the numbers are likely representative of broader trends in the area. Pocatello is one of only five sites in the entire country currently showing very high COVID levels in wastewater, making it a significant outlier not just within Idaho but nationally. The readings are striking not only because they stand in sharp contrast to the low COVID activity being reported locally in terms of actual cases and hospitalizations, but because the rest of Idaho is showing low to very low COVID levels at its other four wastewater testing sites. With COVID activity low in most areas of the country as of early June, Pocatello’s very high reading stands out as one of the most elevated in the nation. “Very high” is the CDC’s most severe wastewater classification, indicating the highest measurable concentration of COVID viral material in the wastewater system. Wastewater monitoring can detect the virus spreading from person to person within a community earlier than clinical testing and before sick people go to their doctor or hospital — and it can also detect infections in people who have no symptoms at all. Idaho currently has five locations that test wastewater for COVID-19. Two are in Ada County, one is in Latah County and one is in Kootenai County. Pocatello — the county seat and largest city in Bannock County — is the fifth testing site and currently the only one in the state showing very high levels. The disconnect between the wastewater data and the reported case numbers is puzzling but not necessarily alarming — at least not yet. Southeastern Idaho has reported approximately 115 COVID cases since mid-November 2025, with fewer than 10 cases reported in all of May. Local health officials are currently characterizing COVID activity in the region as low. No surge in hospitalizations has been reported. That gap between high wastewater readings and low reported cases isn’t unusual and can happen for several reasons. People infected with COVID shed the virus in their feces — sometimes for days before developing symptoms or ever seeking testing. Historical trends show that when COVID levels increase in wastewater, COVID infections in the community typically increase five to seven days later. In other words, wastewater is an early warning system — not a current case count. The low case numbers may also simply reflect the reality that fewer people are getting tested for COVID than during the height of the pandemic. Home tests don’t get reported, and many people with mild symptoms never seek medical care. The COVID circulating in the community may be more widespread than the official case count suggests. Pocatello’s elevated readings are also coming at a time when COVID is quietly on the rise nationally. As of early June 2026, the CDC has noted it remains possible that there could be larger increases this summer, particularly if a variant that the immune system no longer recognizes becomes more common. Summer COVID spikes have become a regular pattern in recent years, with the highest levels typically concentrated in the Western United States. The good news, public health experts note, is that deaths and hospitalizations have been gradually declining across the U.S. each season even as COVID continues to circulate. Local health officials have not yet issued any precautions to Southeast Idaho residents in response to the very high COVID levels in Pocatello’s wastewater. But given the elevated wastewater readings, local residents should take some basic precautions — particularly those who are elderly, immunocompromised or have underlying health conditions. Staying current on COVID vaccinations and updated boosters is the most important step. Those at higher risk should consult with their healthcare provider about whether additional precautions make sense for their situation. Masking in crowded indoor settings is worth considering, particularly when visiting vulnerable family members. If you feel sick, test before attending gatherings. COVID tests remain widely available at pharmacies and clinics throughout the region. Improving ventilation in indoor spaces when possible also reduces risk. The very high wastewater reading in Pocatello is an anomaly that warrants monitoring. Whether it signals a coming uptick in cases — as wastewater data historically has — or represents a temporary spike that recedes without a corresponding wave of illness remains to be seen. Increased COVID wastewater levels indicate a higher risk of infection, but the data alone cannot tell us how many people are infected or predict with certainty what comes next. What public health officials and residents can do is watch the numbers closely, take reasonable precautions and remember that the wastewater system is telling us something — even if we’re not entirely sure yet what that something means. For up-to-date COVID wastewater data, visit the CDC’s National Wastewater Surveillance System at cdc.gov/nwss. Southeastern Idaho Public Health can be reached at 208-233-9080. entry.title: Idaho Surface Water Coalition says severely needed water supply ‘simply is not there’
entry.summary: An irrigation system provides water to East Idaho farm land in this file photo. Originally published June 12 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. With farmers entering an important part of the irrigation season, members of the Idaho Surface Water Coalition say they are reducing water deliveries and usage due to a historically low snowpack, a depleted aquifer and drought emergency. The water that farmers and communities need “simply is not there,” the Idaho Surface Water Coalition said in an announcement Thursday that details how members of the coalition are reducing their own water by up to 33% compared to normal water allocations. “Idaho is in a bad situation,” Idaho Surface Water Coalition Chairman Alan Hansten said in the announcement. “The snow never fell this past winter, so now we are dealing with one of the most challenging water years in generations, with the consequences already stacking up.” “Our members are facing deep cuts to each of their water supplies, which in turn is leaving everyone who relies on our water working hard to stretch their supply,” Hansten added. “Farmers are already having to decide what fields to abandon to make it through the summer. The impacts of this very real drought already equate to lost crops and lost income and will be felt in our towns, our businesses, our grocery stores and around kitchen tables across Idaho and our nation.” The Idaho Surface Water Coalition was formed in 2005 by a group of canal companies and irrigation districts. Collectively, the coalition’s members are responsible for delivering water from the Snake River to about 550,000 acres of farmland in Idaho, the coalition said. The water coalition said the water shortfall is forcing the Twin Falls Canal Co. to reduce its water delivery by 33.3% this year, while the North Side Canal Co. and American Falls Reservoir District No. 2 are each reducing their water by 20% this year. Additionally, the Minidoka Irrigation District is reducing its water delivery by 15% this year, while the Milner Irrigation District is reducing water by 12%. As a result, in one instance, a farmer who receives water from Twin Falls Canal Co. is now chopping grain crops early to sell for use as cattle feed rather than let the crop fail later on due to water shortages, the Idaho Surface Water Coalition said. “It’s important that our fellow Idahoans understand the constraints our canal companies, our irrigation districts, our farmers, our ranchers and all water users are facing,” Hansten said. “Every water user is being asked to do more with less, and they are responding with the same resilience that has always defined Idahoans. The path forward is uncertain, but we will continue to work together to do what is best for the state of Idaho.” Coalition members said that while surface water users are feeling the most immediate impact of the water shortage, drought, low snowpack levels and a depleted Eastern Snake Plain Aquifer affect all water users. Snowpack levels are important because when the snow melts in the spring, it flows down from the mountains to fill the rivers and reservoirs with water that farmers depend on, the coalition said. entry.title: How an Idaho state agency stores decades of old government records and artifacts
entry.summary: Idaho State Historical Society Reference Archivist Owen Prout walks through the agency’s vault of historical records on May 21, 2026. Originally published June 15 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. The first few pages of Idaho’s original state Constitution are tattered. Red ink has bled through — a consequence of state archivists deciding to laminate the first few pages in the ‘70s. But even though the state’s foundational legal document is showing wear, it’s available for the public to see — like thousands of other state government records and donated records that the Idaho State Historical Society houses. The original copy of the Idaho state Constitution, with bleeding red ink and tattered pages. As the state shifts away from aging storage facilities elsewhere in Boise, the State Historical Society is building an expanded 50,000-square-foot storage facility at its headquarters in Boise near the Old Idaho Penitentiary, a prison that operated during Idaho’s territorial days and is now open to the public for tours. That new facility, set to be finished in December, will house physical artifacts and tens of thousands of feet of physical records that the agency houses for other state agencies. “Everything we do here is to provide access and to preserve,” said Angie Davis, an outreach and collections archivist with the State Historical Society. But those two values, she added, “often are at odds with each other, because the more access you have to a thing, the more at risk potentially a thing can be.” The state’s Constitution gets checked out so often that staff with the agency made several other copies of it — including a digitally scanned version of the state Constitution, where those red smudges show up. But “if somebody insists on seeing the original Constitution, we will pull it and just take very special care of it,” Davis said. The Idaho State Historical Society, a state government agency that runs the state’s museum, has for decades cared for many records in the government’s possession and donated by private individuals. First founded as a nonprofit in 1881, the organization became a state agency in 1907. Idaho State Historical Society Executive Director Janet Gallimore reflects on her agency’s mission in the Lincoln Reading Room, where members of the public can access historical records. Since Idaho nuclear downwinders became eligible for federal compensation for sickness after nuclear bomb tests, hundreds have turned to the Idaho State Historical Society and other libraries to find records that prove they or their families lived in Idaho decades ago. But some Idahoans might not know about the wealth of records — like decades of old newspapers, including from abandoned mining towns; city records; and records from Idaho’s territorial days — that the state agency stores and makes publicly accessible. Idaho State Historical Society Executive Director Janet Gallimore said history organizations are among the most trusted in the U.S. because they house original materials. So, she said her staff are cautious to stick to the facts. “We’re very careful that when we do interpretation, that we are speaking from the evidence that we have, and we don’t take a stand one way or the other about the facts,” she said. “It’s just — these are the facts, and they provide context for where we are today. But we don’t try to influence people’s perspective. We try to provide context for where we are.” At the entry to the Idaho State Archives‘ office, there’s an exhibit dedicated to Lincoln — built largely with materials donated by former Idaho Attorney General David Leroy. In the 1960s, archivists with the Idaho State Historical Society started preserving copies of newspapers from across the state. Now, the historical society says it has over 40,000 rolls of microfilm — essentially scanned physical archives of newspapers — dating back to 1863. Newspaper articles between 1836 and 1925 can be accessed digitally through the Idaho Digital Newspaper Project. The historical society also supplies public libraries and universities with microfilm. Idaho State Historical Society Outreach and Collections Archivist Angie Davis thumbs through the Idaho Organic Act, an 1863 congressional bill that established a territorial government for Idaho. And the agency has reams of early documents of Idaho’s history, like a copy of the congressional bill that established a territorial government for Idaho, which is riddled with notes along the margins; and a copy of former U.S. President Abraham Lincoln’s order appointing Idaho’s first territorial governor, William H. Wallace. The agency was also donated archival plans for the construction of the Idaho State Capitol building by the firm Hummel Architects. But if the Idaho State Historical Society doesn’t have what you’re looking for, staff often try to help you track it down elsewhere, Davis said.

entry.title: Idaho farmers are hoping Congress can get updated farm bill across finish line
entry.summary: Idaho farmers are hoping Congress will pass an updated farm bill to update safety-net, conservation, financing and other programs. The bill was passed by the U.S. House and is under consideration in the U.S. Senate. Idaho farmers are hoping Congress will pass an updated farm bill to update safety-net, conservation, financing and other programs. Originally published June 11 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. Many of Idaho’s farmers and producers are dealing with skyrocketing costs to do business, but many of the nation’s support systems for them are outdated. “We have pressure from the cost of fertilizer, the increased cost of fuels, the effects of tariffs on equipment and parts that we need to buy for our operations, and at the same time, the prices that we receive … it hasn’t increased in line with the cost of production,” said Jamie Kress, an East Idaho dryland farmer and president of the National Association of Wheat Growers. Kress and other members of Idaho’s agricultural industry are advocating for Congress to approve an updated farm bill — a massive piece of federal legislation that addresses policies such as food assistance like the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, low-interest farm loans, crop insurance, research, conservation programs, and rural development. The 2026 bill re-authorizes or increases funding for some of these programs, such as assistance for farmers like crop loss programs and financial assistance for purchasing agricultural lands and incentives for conservation programs. The U.S. House of Representatives passed a 2026 farm bill, which would update the policies and extend them for five years. Idaho’s Republican U.S. Reps. Mike Simpson and Russ Fulcher voted in favor of the updated bill, which passed in a 224-200 vote at the end of April. It’s now under consideration in the U.S. Senate. “I regularly hear from farmers, ranchers, and producers in Idaho, and their main concern over the years has been when Congress will reauthorize the Farm Bill,” Simpson said in an emailed statement. “Thanks to a Republican White House, Senate, and House, we have officially delivered on our promise to provide certainty to those who feed our nation. While this legislation supports and invests in our rural communities, it also strengthens our national security by protecting our domestic food supply.” The last major update to the bill was in 2018, and had typically been updated every five years. In recent years, Congress has approved short-term extensions. “The biggest challenge of operating in an outdated farm bill is just uncertainty in agriculture,” Kress said. “A lot of our decisions are very forward-looking. There’s very little that we do in an impulsive manner, or just thinking about tomorrow … so that not knowing what parameters we’ll be operating under long-term is really difficult.” The 976-page bill approved by the House touches many different agricultural programs, as well as others, such as rural broadband access and telemedicine access. Braden Jensen, governmental affairs director at the Idaho Farm Bureau Federation, said the organization appreciates the broad spectrum of provisions in the bill because they cover Idaho’s broad spectrum of agricultural products. “We recognize that Idaho agriculture is very diverse, growing everything from potatoes, sugar beets, wheat, our hay, our dairy products, cattle, specialty crops, we have a little bit of everything,” Jensen said. “So because of that diversity, really all these different titles in the farm bill matter to our producers.” Jensen said the new bill added specialty crops, such as hops, seed crops and potatoes, to risk management programs. Additionally the bill increases borrowing limits on special agricultural loans to reflect increasing costs to expand or start new operations, he said. Kress, the wheat grower, also highlighted the funding of a program to create and expand export markets outside the U.S., and another that allows groups to share the costs of overseas marketing and promotional activities. In 2025, Idaho agriculture exports generated about $3 billion in receipts, the Idaho Capital Sun previously reported. Most of Idaho’s exports go to Canada and Mexico. Idaho wheat growers export roughly half the state’s annual wheat production, Kress said. The Gem State typically ranks in the top 5 in the nation for wheat production. “The trade title (of the farm bill) is very important to our industry,” Kress said. She also highlighted that the bill not only important to the industry, but also consumers of American food products.

entry.title: Legislature closes session marked by steep budget cuts
entry.summary: A record 840 people attended Stampede for the Cure’s Pink on… The Boise City Council voted 4-1 to reconsider the 292-unit … All is quiet under the Idaho State Capitol dome after the close of the 2026 legislative session, Friday, April 3, 2026. Idaho democrats, Rep. Steve Berch, Rep. Ilana Rubel, and Sen. Melissa Wintrow, gather to discuss the 2026 legislative session during a press conference at the Idaho State Capitol, Friday, April 3, 2026. Idaho Democratic Rep. Steve Berch, joined by Rep. Ilana Rubel, and Sen. Melissa Wintrow, discusses the 2026 legislative session during a press conference at the Idaho State Capitol, Friday, April 3, 2026. Idaho Democratic Rep. Steve Berch discusses the results of the 2026 legislative session during a press conference at the Idaho State Capitol, Friday, April 3, 2026. BOISE — Idaho lawmakers announced the close of the 2026 legislative session on Thursday evening after passing through the final gamut of budget bills in a session that saw more than 800 bills, memorials and resolutions drafted. Unlike the previous legislative session that brought more than $400 million in tax cuts, this year was framed from the outset of the session as a “belt tightening” year. Before this year’s session even began, Gov. Brad Little this past fall ordered 3% agency holdbacks. When unveiling his “Enduring Idaho” plan at the start of this session, Little made clear the state would be replacing its previous years marked by large budget surpluses with tens of millions in cuts to transportation funding, Medicaid and other state services. With these overarching budget constraints coloring this year’s legislative session, much of the say for how the session would be carried out fell upon the Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee (JFAC), which sets budgets for agencies in the state. Though Little, along with his budget director Lori Wolff, maintained through the first half of this year’s session that cuts beyond his 3% order would lead to “unintended consequences,” he ultimately signed into law reductions from JFAC that saw most state agency budgets cut by a further 1% this fiscal year, a reduction separate from coming-year budgets approved by the body that brought ongoing 2% cuts to most state agencies. The budget discussions for both this fiscal year and the next brought repeated back-and-forth between committee members as well as the governor’s office on whether some of the added cuts should be restored. When it came to funding the National Guard’s education reimbursement program and wildfire preparedness, it took several rounds of denials from the body before JFAC ultimately moved to enact partial restorations after a letter from Wolff warned of “long-term structural damage.” The legislature also approved limited restorations to behavioral health programs that were cut by the Idaho Department of Health and Welfare last year. Though not all programs were restored, peer support services — which support individuals dealing with mental health ailments — and assertive community treatment — that provide resources for 24/7 mental illness treatment outside of a hospital setting — saw $30.5 million in restoration funding from state legal settlement funds and federal matching dollars. In other cases, such restorations did not arrive. Little signed into law cuts advanced by JFAC to Idaho’s LAUNCH program, which covers up to $8,000 of tuition and fees at eligible institutions. Though advocating in his budget recommendations to stave off any cuts to the program, the in-demand careers fund was cut by $10 million this fiscal year and cut by $10 million on an ongoing basis starting next fiscal year after awards are obligated. Throughout the session, the JFAC leadership in co-chairs Sen. Scott Grow, R-Eagle, and Rep. Josh Tanner, R-Eagle, said these cuts and others were needed to correct the state’s budget. During a mid-session press conference, they specifically said the governor’s budget recommendations — which arrived with a positive ending balance — were “not structurally sound” and would be putting the state in “jeopardy.” Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee chairman, C. Scott Grow, R-Eagle, interacts with participants during a committee meeting, Wednesday, March 4. According to the latest budget update published at the legislature’s adjournment, the state has a projected bottom line of $106.2 million for this fiscal year and a projected $230 million bottom line for fiscal year 2027. Though budget reductions were the through-line for this session, a variety of non-fiscal bills were also at the forefront, particularly those targeting transgender individuals in the state. With the signing of House Bill 752, the legislature adopted statute criminalizing anyone from “knowingly and willfully” entering a public or private accommodation — including restrooms and locker rooms — that do not align with an individual’s biological sex. The law makes first offenses a misdemeanor and subsequent offenses a felony, A separate bill was passed requiring teachers and health care professionals to notify parents within 72 hours if a child requested them to “facilitate” or “participate” in a social gender transition.

entry.title: Idaho Senate rejects 'tool of weaponization' bill granting AG greater power
Caldwell mayor attempts to veto resolutions, but has no legal authority
Bill requiring local law enforcement to apply for agreements with ICE halted in the Senate
Legislature passes bill making ACHD races partisan and countywide starting in 2028
entry.summary: This photo provided by the Ada County Highway District shows a road managed by the county agency. Starting in 2028, ACHD elections will be partisan and countywide, instead of by district. BOISE — The Idaho Legislature approved a bill Wednesday that would make Ada County Highway District elections partisan and countywide. Similar changes to the body were considered last session before they died in committee. The bill was fast-tracked through the legislature Wednesday, passing out of the Senate in the afternoon and sent to the House for a hearing later that evening. Late Wednesday night, the House passed the legislation in a 47-20 vote. The bill is now poised to cap off a multi-year effort to transform Idaho’s only countywide highway district into a partisan body. Though ACHD would keep its current five sub-district arrangement, commissioners would no longer be elected solely by the residents of their respective district and instead be required to participate in partisan elections with associated primaries after operating as a non-partisan body since its inception. Once signed into law, the changes would go into effect for the 2028 election cycle. In her presentation of the legislation on the Senate floor, Sen. Lori Den Hartog, R-Meridian, said the changes would make ACHD elections mirror those of the Ada County Commissioners, who are required to live in a certain district but are elected by every resident of the county. Den Hartog said the shift to countywide elections would allow for the voices of individuals who travel across the county to be heard by commissioners representing the different sub-districts. With several state highways running through the county, Den Hartog said it was important to consider the regional needs of the county as ACHD projects frequently bump up against the highways under the Idaho Transportation Department’s jurisdiction. “As you think about how that state system interacts with the local system here, it’s really important to consider all of those regional needs and make sure that the voice of everyone in Ada County is heard at the ballot box,” Den Hartog said. Alexis Pickering, vice president of the ACHD commissioner board, testified against the legislation during the Wednesday hearing in the House. For commissioners on the body, the five districts of approximately 100,000 are already double to size of legislative districts in Idaho. Shifting the voter base to Ada County’s population, which exceeds 500,000, would make ACHD commissioners beholden to a population the size of a congressional district while fulfilling part-time positions, Pickering said. Miranda Gold, ACHD commission president, testified on behalf of the commission and similarly spoke against the legislation she perceived as marking a sea change for how the commission operates. When the body was formed with the approval of Ada County voters in the 1970s, Gold said the thinking at the time was countywide elections with sub-district residency requirements would “dilute representation” in parts of the county and change the “tone and tenor of the commission.” ”A candidate living in Kuna would need to campaign in Star, and a commissioner could be elected by 80% of voters who do not live in their district,” Gold said. In introducing partisanship and districtwide elections to the body, commissioners will be turned into “full-time fundraisers,” introducing moneyed interests to these races and politicizing transportation projects, Gold said. Echoing Den Hartog, Rep. Jason Monks, R-Meridian, maintained on the House floor that the changes were simply to mirror the arrangement for the county commissioners. ”We’re trying to say that ACHD operates very similar to your county commissioners,” Monks said, “and they should be treated the same.” Rep. Steve Berch, D-Boise, testified twice against the legislation on the House floor and said rejecting the legislation was a matter of remaining consistent for the legislature. He noted in the past sessions the legislature enacted requirements that city council members in cities of 100,000 or more be elected by and live in particular districts. ”If we want to be consistent, we should be requiring the Ada County Commissioners be elected by the district that they’re in, instead of districtwide,” Berch said. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at .

A tearful Boise City Council declares Trans Day of Visibility

Millions of Americans will travel for 4th of July. Here's how to prepare
entry.summary: First responders recovered the body of a 19-year-old man Mon… BOISE — A two-vehicle crash Sunday night resulted in one fat… Traffic moves westbound along Interstate 84 near the Cole Road overpass. A record number of Americans are expected to travel over this year’s Fourth of July weekend. Tumbling gas prices and a one-of-a-kind Fourth of July are fueling record-setting holiday travel projections. According to AAA Idaho, 72.2 million Americans will travel 50 miles or more from home to ring in this year’s Independence Day celebrations — which will include festivities to honor America’s 250th birthday. Among the masses will be 426,000 Idahoans. The holiday comes as gas prices continue to drop in Idaho and across the U.S. But, as AAA notes, higher rates for airfare, hotel stays and rental cars are tempering year-over-year growth. About 71.8 million Americans traveled last Fourth of July, making this year’s a projection a 0.5% increase. “While it’s another record, it’s only a slight bump from a year ago,” AAA Idaho public affairs director Matthew Conde said. “Economic factors may be slowing the leaps-and-bounds growth we’ve seen since the pandemic, but travelers remain very determined to celebrate the holiday in style.” AAA reports that the nationwide average price for a gallon of gasoline was $3.93 as of Monday — down 62 cents from this time last month. It’s down in Idaho, too, to $4.24, 45 cents less than last month. Idaho still ranks seventh in the nation for most expensive fuel. “Crude oil prices are in a nice freefall. Today, the West Texas Intermediate is trading near $73 per barrel — about $13 less than a week ago and $23 less than a month ago,” Conde said. “Some of the folks who were on the fence about taking a road trip may discover that a last-minute getaway is now much more viable.” AAA advises that the busiest days for travel will be Thursday, July 2, and Sunday, July 5. Of the total projected travelers, 61.4 million will go by car while 5.85 million will fly. In addition to getting an early start to your trip, if possible, AAA reminds travelers to take the following pre-travel precautions: “Pack light, and pack smart,” Conde said. “Bring blankets to view fireworks and have a plan in place for staying hydrated and quickly dousing any sparks that may land on dry grass or your roof.” According to AAA, these are the top destinations for Idahoans over Fourth of July weekend: 8. Yellowstone National Park/Grand Teton National Park Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. Body of missing man recovered from Payette River

Boise State presidential finalist believes ‘Idaho is going places’
entry.summary: The Idaho Black Community Alliance (IBCA) will host two days… BOISE — After a yearlong search, Boise State University has … David Hahn, the sole finalist in the search for Boise State University’s next president, interacts with members of the media during a press conference, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. David Hahn, the sole finalist in the search for Boise State University’s next president, interacts with members of the media during a press conference, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. David Hahn, the sole finalist in the search for Boise State University’s next president, interacts with members of the media during a press conference, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. David Hahn, the sole finalist in the search for Boise State University’s next president, interacts with members of the media during a press conference, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. BOISE — Ten days remain in the countdown to vote on Boise State University’s next president, with David W. Hahn named as the sole finalist for the position. On Tuesday, the 62-year-old Hahn visited BSU’s campus, sporting a blue and orange tie. The presidency would be a departure from his current role as the College of Engineering dean at the University of Arizona. The presidential search has been ongoing since the departure of former BSU President Marlene Tromp, who led the university from 2019 to 2025. The yearlong search has led to the selection of Hahn by the Idaho State Board of Education. As a self-described “empty nester,” Hahn and his wife have three adult children. They enjoy their free time hiking through nature and spending time with their Jack Russell terrier named Ophelia. The move to Boise will be a change of scenery. “It’s a beautiful community,” Hahn said about Boise. “The future here is so bright. I’ve heard many people say it’s a hidden gem that’s not being hidden so much.” The accomplished academic comes from a background in engineering and has held a variety of roles in his 28 years of experience. His career path from researcher to administrator began at the University of Florida, where he arrived as a bright-eyed assistant professor. He soon worked his way up to department head before moving to his current role at the University of Arizona, where he said he has built a strong team. During his time in leadership positions, much of Hahn’s strategy has been contingent on teamwork. “If one builds a culture, if (one) builds a sense of team, builds a sense that we are going places, then everyone wants to be on that team,” Hahn said. David Hahn, the sole finalist in the search for Boise State University’s next president, interacts with members of the media during a press conference, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. His emphasis in research previously led him to work for Sandia National Laboratories and the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. According to a BSU press release, Hahn has published more than 130 research articles and holds 12 U.S. patents. He is also a fellow for the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, Optica and the Society for Applied Spectroscopy. Despite his extensive background in engineering, Hahn noted, his focus as president wouldn’t solely be on STEM. “That will be a part of our strategy, because it’s here to serve the state’s needs and labor force, but, and this is an important one, we will look at this entire campus,” Hahn said. “We have five academic colleges, and every single one of those colleges is important to our success.” Hahn’s decision to come to Idaho was largely based on BSU’s upward trajectory. Since 2022, BSU has had a 10% increase in enrollment, according to previous Idaho Press coverage. “It’s the momentum that this institution has. It’s a national athletic brand, which is huge. ... That’s part of the fabric of the community,” he said. “It’s the potential in a state that is going places, Idaho is going places.”

entry.title: Gov. Little says 'Idaho is prepared' as drought, low snowpack mark start of fire season
entry.summary: Columbia Distributing plans to acquire a minority stake in B… A 68-year-old Nampa woman died Monday after she was struck b… Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. Nampa Fire Protection District Chief Kirk Carpenter, president of the Idaho Fire Chiefs Association, adds to a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. BOISE — Firefighting agencies gathered Tuesday at the Idaho State Capitol for a pre-season wildfire briefing. As fire season enters into its early stages, state leaders underscored that the historically hot and dry conditions bring the potential for a significant and costly fire season. Officials who spoke at Tuesday’s Land Board meeting and subsequent pre-fire season press conference consistently said widespread drought conditions are expected to continue, conducive to a heightened fire season. As of Tuesday — still very early in the fire season — there have been 59 fires across 549 acres, 26 of which were human caused, 13 were natural and 20 have a cause yet to be determined, the Idaho Department of Lands’ (IDL’s) division administrator of operations Jake Strohmeyer said. Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. While the winter months saw near-normal precipitation, warm temperatures resulted in above-normal snowpack only being observed at elevations above 6,000 feet near the panhandle and 7,000 feet in southern Idaho. Jim Wallmann, a Bureau of Land Management meteorologist at the National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC), said these temperatures were the second warmest on record, averaging 6 degrees above normal from December through May. The limited snowpack in Idaho, which was at a record low as of April 1, has impacted the fire fuels in the state, with conditions characterized as “above normal” for much of southern Idaho. After a wet April in southwest Idaho, an abundance of fine fuels, such as tall grasses, have grown in the region, Wallmann said. If the dry and hot conditions continue as expected, these fuels only add to the potential for future wildfires. In some respects, Wallmann said what Idaho is observing is not unique. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, about 60% of the country is experiencing drought conditions. Though these conditions are starting to show signs of improvement in the southeastern U.S., the snow drought in the western U.S. is contributing to worsening drought conditions. The most recent national seasonal drought forecast indicated that drought will persist through much of Idaho at least through to the end of August. In April, the Idaho Department of Water Resources issued an emergency drought declaration for each of the state’s 44 counties. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, Owyhee and Twin Falls counties are experiencing “exceptional drought,” the highest intensity on the drought scale. Though conditions conducive to wildfire are expected to persist over the coming months, officials reiterated IDL was in a positive staffing position before fire season gets into full swing. Across IDL’s 50-engine fleet, Strohmeyer said 36 will be fully staffed, 11 more than the previous fire season. In terms of individual personnel, the department has 260 operational firefighters heading into the fire season, 31 more than last year. He added that 48% of these firefighters have one or more years of fire experience and all of the department’s full-time firefighting positions have been filled. The department is also bringing back a Type 1 helicopter based out of Coeur d’Alene which just came under contract, but is still working to bolster its air resources for the future. While IDL works to restart its helitack program, which provides aerial support for wildfires, the department will be partnering with the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources to bring on their Type 3 helicopter along with their crew. Strohmeyer said this arrangement will allow firefighters to receive needed training for when IDL’s helitack program restarts, potentially as soon as next year. Idaho Department of Lands Director Dustin Miller adds to a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026.

entry.title: Federal judge blocks parts of Idaho bathroom bill from taking effect
entry.summary: Columbia Distributing plans to acquire a minority stake in B… A 68-year-old Nampa woman died Monday after she was struck b… Idaho lawmakers passed a sweeping bathroom ban March 27, 2026, approving legislation that would make it a crime for transgender people to use the bathroom that matches their gender identity, even inside privately owned businesses. The bill that would have criminalized transgender people for using public restrooms consistent with their gender identity will not fully go into effect July 1, after a federal judge granted injunctive relief Tuesday to Idahoans who sued to block the implementation of that aspect of the law. House Bill 752, which Gov. Brad Little signed earlier this spring, labeled the use of bathrooms or changing rooms “designated for use by the opposite biological sex” as a misdemeanor for the first instance and a felony punishable by up to five years’ imprisonment for the second. The American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) and its Idaho chapter, as well as Lambda Legal, represent six transgender Idaho plaintiffs in the case known as Jackson-Edney v. Labrador. The plaintiffs are not challenging the changing room aspect of the legislation, and the injunction blocks the ban from taking effect with regards to single-user public restrooms. “This ruling means trans folks in Idaho can continue participating in public life without the threat of being arrested for using the bathroom,” the ACLU of Idaho’s legal director, Paul Carlos Southwick, said in a statement. “This ruling will relieve that anxiety for our trans friends and neighbors.” Vagueness was one ground on which enforcement of the statute was blocked. “The Court finds that H.B. 752 is unconstitutionally vague because it fails to provide sufficient standards for law enforcement,” Judge Amanda Brailsford’s court order read. For example, the terms “dire need” and “reasonably available” are used in reference to circumstances in which exceptions can be made to the ban, but the legislation’s text does not give definitions for either term. The definition of an individual’s biological sex — whether it is determined by an officer’s estimation of one’s visual appearance or by DNA collection that cannot be mandated without a warrant — was another issue the ruling cast as vague and arbitrary. “Without statutory definitions, narrowing context, or settled legal meanings H.B. 752 invites arbitrary and discriminatory enforcement,” the order reads. “Having concluded that Plaintiffs are likely to succeed on the merits of their vagueness claim, the Court finds irreparable harm will likely occur in the absence of preliminary relief.” The attorney general’s office had argued in favor of HB 752 on the basis of “privacy and safety concerns that have always motivated sex-segregated bathrooms,” as Solicitor General Michael Zarian told the court June 5. Brailsford said that Idaho already has laws addressing misconduct in restrooms, such as those against sexual battery, malicious harassment and indecent exposure. In a Tuesday statement, Attorney General Raúl Labrador said that the ruling was a misinterpretation of HB 752’s intent and the state’s position. “Biological sex is not vague, and neither is the law,” Labrador said. “The good news is that the ruling is narrow. Idaho’s law remains enforceable in most settings, including changing rooms and many restrooms.” The law may still be enforced with respect to multiple-stall restrooms if no single-stall facility is available. Labrador expressed his intention to appeal the decision, which remains in place as the court continues to weigh HB 752’s constitutionality. Six transgender plaintiffs are suing to prevent Idaho''s new bathroom law from being enacted July 1. They are being represented by the ACLU.

entry.title: David W. Hahn named lone finalist to become Boise State University's next president
entry.summary: BOISE — Ten days remain in the countdown to vote on Boise St… BOISE — Locals will have the opportunity to learn more about… David W. Hahn, lone finalist to be the eighth president at Boise State University. BOISE — After a yearlong search, Boise State University has found its new president. The Idaho State Board of Education on Tuesday announced David W. Hahn as the lone finalist to serve as the eighth president of Boise State University. Hahn will succeed Marlene Tromp, who in March 2025 announced she was leaving Boise to become president of the University of Vermont. Jeremiah Shinn, Boise State’s vice president for student affairs and enrollment management, served as interim president, though in April was tabbed by the University of Montana to be its next president. Nancy Glenn, vice president of research and economic development, stepped in as acting president while the search at Boise State continued. Hahn currently serves as the Craig M. Berge Dean of the College of Engineering at the University of Arizona. “After a rigorous and thorough national search, I am confident the State Board has identified the right leader for this moment at Boise State,” David Turnbull, chairman of the Presidential Search Committee and member of the Idaho State Board of Education, said in a press statement. “Dr. Hahn brings exceptional credentials as someone who has consistently built institutions that are stronger and more innovative than when he arrived. Boise State and the state of Idaho are well-served by this selection.” Hahn brings more than 30 years of combined experience in engineering education, research and administration. “Boise State University has exceptional momentum, and my pledge is to continue guiding our contributions in all aspects, from a strong commitment to delivering a high-value and affordable education to our students, innovating in support of state, regional and national research needs, partnering with the communities and industries of Idaho, fostering the winning culture of Bronco Athletics, while successfully navigating the changing landscape of higher education,” Hahn said in a press statement. “This all starts with people and throughout my interactions, I have seen a group fully dedicated to the success of Boise State University and I am honored to join the team.” In accordance with Idaho state law, Hahn was named as the sole finalist for the position, officially beginning a 10-business-day period prior to a formal vote by the Idaho State Board of Education. You can email the Idaho State Board of Education with your feedback at . Trevor Hawes is the executive editor of the Idaho Press. BOISE — Ten days remain in the countdown to vote on Boise State University’s next president, with David W. Hahn named as the sole finalist for… Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! entry.title: Gov. Little vetoes 5 bills so far, including day care and the state budget
entry.summary: Bar, Please! and Ansots Basque Chorizos & Catering recen… Initial motions were made by Boise City Council regarding th… Gov. Brad Little delivers the annual State of the State address in the House chambers of the Idaho State Capitol, Monday, Jan. 12. Little on Wednesday issued five vetoes on bills passed during this year’s legislative session. BOISE — Gov. Brad Little vetoed five bills Wednesday evening regarding legislation impacting a range of issues including day care, crypto kiosks and the state’s budget picture. The series of vetoes, which arrived with respective explanatory letters, are the most Little has issued in a single day since being elected governor in 2018. A key change to Idaho statute in House Bill 758 allowed a day care provider’s own children, when under 5 years of age, to not be counted when the provider is operating out of their own home. This change would have effectively allowed providers to work around the Idaho Department of Health and Welfare’s child-to-staff ratio formulas. “House Bill 758a threatens child safety,” Little stated in his veto letter. “This bill could eliminate licensing and fire inspection requirements for providers. In a fire or emergency evacuation, higher rations and weaker safety requirements would prove catastrophic.” House Bill 674 was passed by the legislature to adjust guidelines around when telecommunication service providers would be permitted to withdraw or discontinue their service to a local area. The bill directed the Idaho Public Utilities Commission (IPUC) to adopt findings from the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) on whether such a provider could discontinue their service. In other words, if the federal authority found a telecommunications discontinuation to be permissible under federal law, the IPUC would be required to align with these findings, regardless of their own analysis. In his veto letter, Little stated it was the role of the state of Idaho, rather than the federal government, to administer telecommunications rules, including when telecommunication corporations elect to withdraw from a service area. Two budgetary bills originating from the Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee (JFAC) were also the subject of vetoes Little signed Wednesday. House Bill 975 was passed by the legislature to allow the state to withhold what would have otherwise been an automatic transfer from the state budget stabilization fund, or primary rainy day fund, to the state general fund. Idaho code states this transfer is to take place automatically when the state’s rainy day fund exceeds 15% of the total general fund for the ending fiscal year. For the fiscal year 2027 transfer, this was based on the projected fiscal year 2026 revenue of $5.5 billion. Based on this revenue number, the rainy day fund’s balance of $880.2 million was $53.7 million beyond the 15% cap. In locking the excess resources down to the budget stabilization fund, Little stated Idaho would be limiting the ability to respond to emerging needs, including those that could arrive in just a few months time. ”My veto of this bill makes additional funds available to pay for what we expect will be an extensive and challenging fire season,” Little stated. House Bill 968 authorized a series of one-time cash transfers to the state’s general fund in a move the JFAC co-chairs said was done to “cushion” the next fiscal year’s budget if the need arose. The legislation notably arrived after JFAC co-chair and Rep. Josh Tanner, R-Eagle, had been a vocal critic of this method of budgeting, which was employed in Little’s January budget recommendations. Early in the session, Tanner described Little’s budget recommendations as “gimmicks” that left the state budget unbalanced. Rather than a total veto of this series of transfers, Little’s employed a “line-item veto” that can be used to strike portions of bills making appropriations, but allow other provisions to pass into law. Little’s veto concerned two portions of the cash transfers. The first pertained to the appropriation of funds from canceled projects under the Permanent Building Fund. Little recommended in his budget such a transfer take place to balance the budget, rather than appropriate the funding to an unrelated account. But because Idaho Code requires appropriations for canceled projects to be redistributed to other capital projects, the transfer was a code violation. “While I support prudent investment in state facilities, this appropriations is inconsistent with the long-term purpose and stability of the Permanent Building Fund,” Little stated in his letter. Little also vetoed a portion of the bill directing a transfer from the 27th payroll fund, which is in place in the for years the state has an additional payroll cycle, as will be the case in 2028.

entry.title: Immigration bills caused big stir this year, but none advanced in Idaho Legislature
entry.summary: Bar, Please! and Ansots Basque Chorizos & Catering recen… Initial motions were made by Boise City Council regarding th… The Idaho Statehouse is seen at sunrise on April 20, 2021, in Boise. Originally published April 8 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. On the last day of the 2026 Idaho legislative session, a Fernwood Republican representative expressed exasperation that the GOP supermajority Legislature had not yet advanced any of the several immigration bills proposed this year. That vote marked the third legislative attempt to require local law enforcement to enter certain agreements with federal immigration authorities, and one of more than two dozen other proposals related to immigration that stalled. “We are a conservative state, and on this issue, we can’t seem to take a step forward,” Rep. Dale Hawkins said on the House floor. “And I got to be honest with you, folks, I don’t understand that.” Idaho Republicans are divided on the issue. Some are pushing for more hardline state-level policies to uphold federal laws. Others say it’s a solely federal issue, cite law enforcement opposition, or don’t want to disrupt Idaho industries that rely on undocumented workers. Sen. Brian Lenney speaks at a hearing in this Feb. 15, 2023, file photo. “The White House is paying attention to Idaho,” Nampa Republican Sen. Brian Lenney told the Idaho Capital Sun, referencing a letter from a White House adviser supporting one of the stalled bills. “And they’re not happy with what they’re seeing and what they saw this legislative session.” In total, there were 14 bills introduced this year — including those that were revised versions of previous legislation — that were related to illegal immigration in some way. The proposals included tracking aggregated data of immigration status of all public school students, verifying nationality and status of every arrested person, creating new crimes for helping undocumented people or a new state-level crime for hiring unauthorized workers. None of them advanced to the governor’s desk for approval. Jaclyn Kettler, a Boise State University political science professor who researches state politics, said divisions are highlighted among Republicans who align more closely with the agriculture industry. The major presence of the agriculture and dairy industries in Idaho — whose leaders have said they rely on immigrant and undocumented labor — has shaped the conversation in Idaho differently, she said. Lenney said he sees the division among different “buckets” of Republicans, including the “MAGA, America-first,” which he said he is in, and “mainstream” and “moderate” Republicans. Lenney, who is in his second, two-year term as a state senator, said he’s always been in the MAGA group, referring to Donald Trump’s slogan “Make America Great Again,” and wants to be a leader on the issue. He sees the other buckets being more reluctant to make state-level changes. Coeur d’Alene Republican Jordan Redman said that, growing up in North Idaho, he wasn’t as exposed as much to industries like dairies. For him, immigration is a “law and order” issue, and he’s pushed for more widespread enforcement on employers who hire undocumented workers. However, he recognized that some of his Republican colleagues don’t have the same perspective. Rep. Jordan Redman, R-Coeur d’Alene, on the Idaho House floor on March 25, 2024. “We all shape our views politically from life experience,” Redman said. “And it’s probably from the life experience of some of the different parts of the state, where they still utilize the undocumented labor force, see it as a big issue if you verify documentation. I think it’s probably a dividing application of people using different industries.” The division isn’t unique to Idaho, as Republicans in other red states pushed back against work authorization verification requirements this year as well, Stateline reported. Some of those in Idaho who opposed various proposals said it was more because of execution of the bills, rather than their intention. Lenney told the Sun he and others were “shocked” by the strong opposition by Idaho law enforcement against many of the proposals this year.
