Bill requiring local law enforcement to apply for agreements with ICE halted in the Senate
entry.summary: Initial motions were made by Boise City Council regarding th… Columbia Distributing plans to acquire a minority stake in B… A bill that would have required law enforcement agencies in Idaho to apply for cooperative agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement has been halted in the Senate. BOISE — The Idaho Senate has halted the advancement of a bill that would have required law enforcement agencies to apply for cooperative agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Following the bill’s passage out of the House on Thursday, Sen. Mark Harris, R-Soda Springs, motioned to not concur with the House’s amendments to the legislation, preventing it from advancing on grounds that it violates Senate Rule 28(D). Under this rule, a bill cannot be amended to remove or incorporate provisions of another bill already pending in the Senate. Senate Bill 1247 was originally the Idaho e-verify act to require all public agencies to implement the federal e-verify program, which confirms the eligibility to work in the United States for new employees. The House amendment retooled the bill entirely to effectively swap a pending ICE cooperation bill with the stalled e-verify legislation. By unanimous consent, the Senate opted to not advance the reworked bill. Under the amended Senate bill, city and county law enforcement would be required to apply for cooperation agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. These arrangements — known as section 287(g) memorandums of agreement — allow local law enforcement officers to be trained and deputized under programs that permit limited enforcement of federal immigration law. These programs function according to three separate models. A jail enforcement model allows officers to identify and process removable undocumented immigrants with either pending or active criminal charges. A task force model allows officers to have limited immigration enforcement authority in the performance of their routine police duties and participate in ICE-led task forces. A warrant service officer model allows officers to serve executive administrative warrants on undocumented immigrants in custody. Under each of these models, individual officers would need to participate in training and/or receive certification from ICE in order to be deputized to enforce federal immigration law. In cases where a law enforcement agency is “unable” to enter into a section 287(g) agreement, the law enforcement agency is required to publish a statement on the reasons for its inability to enter into an agreement as well as the alternative efforts made to cooperate with “enforcement and removal operation of immigration and customs enforcement.” According to the ICE website, a variety of sheriffs offices such as Kootenai and Owyhee counties are already engaged in some capacity with at least one of these three agreements. At the direction of Gov. Brad Little, the Idaho State Police also entered a 287(g) agreement last year to transport unauthorized immigrants convicted of crimes to deportation centers. This bill is a new version of House Bill 659, which passed out of the House last month before being held in Senate committee in a one-vote margin. In the public hearing before the House and the Senate, the measure was opposed by the police chiefs and county sheriffs who testified. Canyon County Sheriff Kieran Donahue, who testified on the previous bill on behalf of the Idaho Sheriffs Association, said sheriffs in the state were "absolutely opposed" to the legislation due to it mandating what sheriffs can and can''t do. He highlighted law enforcement would be on the hook for backfill and other expenses associated with officers departing for ICE-led training and would result in fewer officers to participate in daily patrol duties. In addition to testifying on previous renditions of the bill, Ada County Sheriff Matt Clifford issued a statement last week underscoring his office already cooperates with ICE on a daily basis to share arrest lists and comply with lawful requests to hold individuals for deportation. Clifford also emphasized that he has been able to align or decouple his agency depending on when federal objectives align with the needs of the county. "In my role as sheriff, I have made decisions to end partnerships with federal agencies when their demands or direction were overreaching and no longer in the best interest of Ada County," Clifford stated. "A forced relationship with ICE under a mandated program gives me no such option when participation begins to erode what is best for our community." During debate on the House floor, Rep. Britt Raybould, R-Rexburg, questioned why the legislature was seeking to force law enforcement’s hand in the matter when agencies are already sharing relevant information with ICE or participating in 287(g) agreements themselves.

entry.title: Legislature passes bill making ACHD races partisan and countywide starting in 2028
entry.summary: The polls are open for some Treasure Valley residents lookin… BOISE — Boiseans signaled Tuesday that there continues to be… This photo provided by the Ada County Highway District shows a road managed by the county agency. Starting in 2028, ACHD elections will be partisan and countywide, instead of by district. BOISE — The Idaho Legislature approved a bill Wednesday that would make Ada County Highway District elections partisan and countywide. Similar changes to the body were considered last session before they died in committee. The bill was fast-tracked through the legislature Wednesday, passing out of the Senate in the afternoon and sent to the House for a hearing later that evening. Late Wednesday night, the House passed the legislation in a 47-20 vote. The bill is now poised to cap off a multi-year effort to transform Idaho’s only countywide highway district into a partisan body. Though ACHD would keep its current five sub-district arrangement, commissioners would no longer be elected solely by the residents of their respective district and instead be required to participate in partisan elections with associated primaries after operating as a non-partisan body since its inception. Once signed into law, the changes would go into effect for the 2028 election cycle. In her presentation of the legislation on the Senate floor, Sen. Lori Den Hartog, R-Meridian, said the changes would make ACHD elections mirror those of the Ada County Commissioners, who are required to live in a certain district but are elected by every resident of the county. Den Hartog said the shift to countywide elections would allow for the voices of individuals who travel across the county to be heard by commissioners representing the different sub-districts. With several state highways running through the county, Den Hartog said it was important to consider the regional needs of the county as ACHD projects frequently bump up against the highways under the Idaho Transportation Department’s jurisdiction. “As you think about how that state system interacts with the local system here, it’s really important to consider all of those regional needs and make sure that the voice of everyone in Ada County is heard at the ballot box,” Den Hartog said. Alexis Pickering, vice president of the ACHD commissioner board, testified against the legislation during the Wednesday hearing in the House. For commissioners on the body, the five districts of approximately 100,000 are already double to size of legislative districts in Idaho. Shifting the voter base to Ada County’s population, which exceeds 500,000, would make ACHD commissioners beholden to a population the size of a congressional district while fulfilling part-time positions, Pickering said. Miranda Gold, ACHD commission president, testified on behalf of the commission and similarly spoke against the legislation she perceived as marking a sea change for how the commission operates. When the body was formed with the approval of Ada County voters in the 1970s, Gold said the thinking at the time was countywide elections with sub-district residency requirements would “dilute representation” in parts of the county and change the “tone and tenor of the commission.” ”A candidate living in Kuna would need to campaign in Star, and a commissioner could be elected by 80% of voters who do not live in their district,” Gold said. In introducing partisanship and districtwide elections to the body, commissioners will be turned into “full-time fundraisers,” introducing moneyed interests to these races and politicizing transportation projects, Gold said. Echoing Den Hartog, Rep. Jason Monks, R-Meridian, maintained on the House floor that the changes were simply to mirror the arrangement for the county commissioners. ”We’re trying to say that ACHD operates very similar to your county commissioners,” Monks said, “and they should be treated the same.” Rep. Steve Berch, D-Boise, testified twice against the legislation on the House floor and said rejecting the legislation was a matter of remaining consistent for the legislature. He noted in the past sessions the legislature enacted requirements that city council members in cities of 100,000 or more be elected by and live in particular districts.

entry.title: JFAC votes to withhold more than $50 million from general fund, opting for larger bottom line
entry.summary: Bar, Please! and Ansots Basque Chorizos & Catering recen… Initial motions were made by Boise City Council regarding th… Legislators at work on the floor of the Idaho Senate as the end of the legislative session draws near Tuesday, March 31, 2026. BOISE — Throughout the legislative session, leadership on the Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee has framed 2% cuts to state agencies for the next fiscal year as a necessary action to ensure the state’s budget was in a “structurally sound” position. On Wednesday, however, the committee voted to have tens of million in funds held in the state’s Budget Stabilization Fund that would have otherwise been automatically transferred to bolster the coming year’s bottom line. Existing code outlining the function of the Budget Stabilization Fund, also known as Idaho’s rainy day fund, states the balance shall not exceed 15% of the total general fund for the ending fiscal year. For fiscal year 2027, this amount would be based upon the fiscal year 2026 revenue, which JFAC projected at $5.51 billion. If exceeding this 15% threshold, the state controller is directed to transfer this money to the general fund unless the legislature acts to reduce the amount of the transfer, which JFAC initiated on Wednesday. This stabilization fund balance currently sits at $880.2 million. When compared to a projected general fund revenue of $5.51 billion for fiscal year 2026, this amounts to a difference of $53.7 million beyond the 15% cap. “It makes no sense to me that we would set aside code and not put this money in the general fund, especially when we have cut budgets to the bone and then some,” Sen. Janie Ward-Engelking, D-Boise, said. This amount of budget stabilization funds that would have otherwise been moved automatically is well above the $30.1 million in ongoing 2% cuts JFAC initially approved for most state agencies in fiscal year 2027. Though several restorations, including those for wildfire suppression, were fully or partially enacted by the body in the time since, this was not true for every agency. In the case of Idaho’s colleges and universities, JFAC voted against restoring a 2% ongoing cut, or $7.3 million general fund reduction for these institutions. “We should have been budgeting for this on the green sheet from the start of session,” Keith Bybee, budget and policy division manager of the Legislative Services Office said in reference to the daily budget update form issued by LSO. “… With the revenue number that the legislature adopted, that would have triggered an automatic transfer from the budget stabilization fund in 2027 back to the general fund.” Bybee said this has not been an issue until the last two sessions when the legislature saw hundreds of millions of dollars in budget surpluses that resulted in this stabilization fund hitting the 15% cap. These were both years that did not see broad agency cuts to state agencies, as was the case this legislative session. Sen. Melissa Wintrow, D-Boise, questioned why the body wouldn’t want the excess funds automatically transferring to the general fund, given the budget constraints of this session. Bybee responded it was a policy decision by the body to leave excess funds on the bottom line for the coming fiscal year. Last week JFAC advanced a series of cash transfers that will only take place if the state’s general fund balance falls below $150 million. JFAC co-chairs Sen. Scott Grow, R-Eagle, and Rep. Josh Tanner, R-Eagle, said the intent is to ensure the state has a “cushion” heading into next year. Sen. Jim Woodward, R-Sagle, took issue with the committee’s course of action and said the 15% cap on the budget stabilization fund was in place to ensure current taxpayers have their dollars appropriately utilized in the present. ”We are asking today’s taxpayers to take care of tomorrow’s taxpayers’ problems,” Woodward said. “The people who are putting money in this budget stabilization fund today may not be Idahoans when it gets spent.” Despite reservations voiced by several committee members on not allowing for the automatic transfer to take place, JFAC voted 16-4 to hold the excess budget stabilization funds for the next fiscal year. Ward-Engelking, Wintrow, Rep. Brooke Green, D-Boise, and Rep. Rod Furniss, R-Rigby, each voted against. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now!

entry.title: A tearful Boise City Council declares Trans Day of Visibility
entry.summary: Initial motions were made by Boise City Council regarding th… Columbia Distributing plans to acquire a minority stake in B… Boise Mayor Lauren McLean responds to the crowd at the Boise Pride parade in 2023. A heaviness set the tone of Tuesday’s Boise City Council meeting, on the heels of anti-LGBTQ+ legislation being signed into Idaho law, as Boise declared March 31 as the Trans Day of Visibility. Council members spoke through tears at the special meeting as they reflected on the importance of allyship following the passage of a flag ban earlier that day. After over a decade of the Pride flag flying outside Boise City Hall, Gov. Brad Little signed an amended law to restrict what flags cities and counties can display. The bill, HB 561, comes after previous attempts to outlaw the Pride flag on city and county property in the 2025 legislative session. Skirting around the ban, Mayor Lauren McLean and the city council continued flying the flag due to a lack of enforcement mechanisms in the 2025 legislation. The updated legislation will now enforce a $2,000 fine for each day an unauthorized flag is displayed. Acceptable flags include the U.S. flag, state flags, military flags, the POW/MIA flag, flags of Indian tribes, and country flags for special occasions. On Tuesday, McLean spoke to a packed room. Over the past year, she has stood firmly in the city’s right to show support for the LGBTQ+ community. “I hope that knowing that you are loved, seen and welcomed by this city enables you to participate in the fabric of this community,” McLean said. She described the day as a sad one for the people who call Boise home, but welcomed the Trans Day of Visibility proclamation as a way to show support for a marginalized population. Tuesday also saw the signing of HB 752, a bill that criminalizes the use of public facilities that do not align with a person’s “biological sex.” A repeat offender will be subject to a felony charge. “They are seeking to divide us by targeting the most vulnerable among us,” McLean said about lawmakers. “There’s little we can do at the state and national level, but there is much that can be done here at home.” McLean choked up as she discussed the future of Idaho’s next generation, describing mothers who have to watch their children leave the state. She seemingly referred to the strained state of Idaho’s obstetrics care and abortion rights. Idaho has some of the most restrictive abortion laws in the nation. With the overturning of Roe v. Wade by the U.S. Supreme Court in 2022, an abortion trigger ban quickly went into effect. “I thought about my own kid, and their questions of whether or not their friends are welcome here,” McLean said. “Whether as a woman, my daughter will be able to have children here in a healthy and safe way.” Council Member Colin Nash reflected on his previous experience as a representative for District 16 in the Idaho House. He resigned in 2024, citing his intent to focus more on city council. “Often in the legislature, it felt like casting your pearls before swine,” Nash said. Nash spoke about his wife’s cousin, Shay, who killed herself a few years ago after a lack of acceptance for identifying with the LGBTQ+ community. As a representative at the time Nash wanted to share her story in the legislature, but, as he expected from previous experience, “it just didn’t resonate, their hearts were hardened.” “Shay was a lovely soul,” Nash said, speaking through tears. “... I’m not asking you to empathize, but if someone feels something so deeply that they would go to those ends, can we just give them a little bit more understanding? A little bit more sympathy?” Sporting a rainbow sticker on her blazer, Council Pro Tem Kathy Corless spoke on the importance of visibility. While she identifies herself as a longtime LGBTQ+ ally, Corless said she has realized a need to be more transparent.

New report finds tax cuts benefit highest-income Idaho households most
entry.summary: CALDWELL — A Caldwell man faces felony charges after he alle… A Canyon County man may spend the rest of his life in prison… The Capitol dome stands against the Boise foothills, Tuesday, April 7, 2026. Originally published June 18 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. A new report published this month by a Boise-based nonprofit organization found that state and federal income tax cuts have benefited the highest-income Idaho households the most, while low-income families are actually paying more. The Idaho Center for Fiscal Policy published the findings in the new report, “How federal and state tax cuts shift costs onto Idaho families and communities.” The report’s author, Policy Analyst May Roberts, tracked $4.5 billion in annual state and federal income tax cuts that Idaho households have received since 2018. The report found that 73% of the benefits of those cuts went to the top 20% of households, those that earn $143,000 or more each year. The report also found that Idahoans with the lowest incomes – those earning less than $29,200 annually – saw their taxes increase by an average of $96 per year. Since 2021, the Idaho Legislature has enacted several tax cuts that have reduced state revenue. Here are a few examples: The increase in taxes among the lowest-income earners was due, in part, to the Idaho Legislature allowing the state child tax credit to expire and the federal One Big Beautiful Bill Act not extending premium tax credits that helped Idaho families and small business owners afford health insurance, the report stated. “Tax cuts can be a valuable tool for helping families make ends meet, but it is important to look at who benefits and who bears the costs,” Roberts said in a written statement. “A fair tax system can provide meaningful tax relief while maintaining the essential services Idaho families rely on. When tax cuts significantly reduce the resources available for public services, the costs do not disappear — they are shifted onto families and local communities through higher property taxes, rising tuition and fees, reduced services and growing barriers to accessing healthcare and food assistance.” Sen. Scott Grow, a Republican from Eagle who serves as co-chair of the Idaho Legislature’s Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee, supports the tax cuts. “I am a fiscal conservative,” Grow said. “As (certified public accountants), we try to help our clients to make sure they pay a fair amount of tax and not overly pay. What we are hearing from our constituents is that they are concerned about high taxes, whether it be property tax or income tax. We try to be very fiscally conservative. I believe in limited government. We need to fund the government appropriately, and I feel like we do that.” The report also found that the combined $4.5 billion in tax cuts exceeds the Idaho Legislature’s fiscal year 2026 state funding for K-12 public education, colleges and universities and health and human services. The report said that for fiscal year 2026, there was $2.2 billion in state funding for K-12 public education, $1.2 billion for health and human services and $492 million for colleges and universities. After the Idaho Legislature cut funding for colleges in universities in fiscal year 2026 and fiscal year 2027, the Idaho State Board of Education approved tuition increases, Idaho Education News reported. “These examples illustrate how state-level budget cuts can shift costs to local governments and directly onto Idahoans,” the Idaho Center for Fiscal Policy’s new report states. More information about the Idaho Center for Fiscal Policy and its research is available online. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left.

entry.title: Canyon County man to serve decades in prison for child sex abuse material crime
entry.summary: CALDWELL — A Caldwell man faces felony charges after he alle… BOISE — The U.S. Marshals Service’s Greater Idaho Fugitive T… A Canyon County man may spend the rest of his life in prison for distributing child sex abuse material. Daniel Whiteley, age 47, was sentenced on June 8 to serve a sentence of 40 years to life after pleading guilty to knowingly distributing child sex abuse material, or CSAM, the Idaho Attorney General’s Office announced. According to online court records, Whiteley — a registered sex offender — was initially charged with one count of distributing CSAM, five counts of possessing or accessing CSAM, one count of destruction of evidence and a persistent violator enhancement. The charges were filed between August 2024 and September 2025. Whiteley pleaded not guilty to the charges before ultimately agreeing to a plea deal, pleading guilty to the distributing CSAM charge, online court records show. The attorney general’s office noted that the crime to which Whiteley pleaded guilty is punishable by up to 30 years in prison; however, Whiteley’s persistent violator enhancement allowed for a longer sentence. Whiteley pleaded guilty to rape in 2000. The AG’s office stated that the investigation into Whiteley started after the Internet Crimes Against Children (ICAC) Unit received a CyberTip from the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children. The tip was generated by a popular social media platform and indicated that a user — subsequently identified as Whiteley — shared child sexual abuse material with another user via direct messaging. Whiteley’s bio on the social media platform read: “46-year-old single m[ale] from Idaho, just another dirty old man perv.” Law enforcement obtained and executed a search warrant for Whiteley’s residence and, the AG’s office states, upon entering the residence, law enforcement saw Whiteley come out of a room where officers found a recently broken laptop. A digital forensic analysis of the laptop located over 1,000 files of child sexual abuse material. The children in the files ranged from approximately 2 years old to teenagers and many were being sexually abused by adults. “This is exactly the kind of predator who has no business walking free in our communities,” Attorney General Raúl Labrador stated. “Every file on that laptop was a real child being abused. My office will keep working with local law enforcement to find these criminals, build strong cases, and put them away for as long as the law allows.” In her sentencing judgment, District Judge Cheri C. Copsey reasoned that “Whiteley is not amenable to treatment, citing particular concern with the number of files found on Whiteley’s computer and his lack of accountability,” the AG’s office said. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! entry.title: Garden City gets $2M for drinking water
entry.summary: A Canyon County man may spend the rest of his life in prison… CALDWELL — A vegetation fire in northwest Caldwell prompted … The “Welcome to Garden City” sign is pictured in this 2022 file photo. GARDEN CITY — The Idaho Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) has awarded more than $2 million to Garden City to help the city invest in drinking water infrastructure. The $2,179,100 in funding will “support the design and drilling of a new drinking water well free of emerging contaminants,” according to a DEQ release. The release notes that emerging contaminants “are substances that may pose newly identified or re-emerging risks to human health or the environment. Examples include per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), pharmaceuticals and personal care products, and microplastics.” The loan carries a simple 0% interest rate and $2,179,100 in principal forgiveness, the release states. The monies are from DEQ’s State Revolving Loan Fund and capitalized annually by grants from the United States Environmental Protection Agency’s Emerging Contaminants program, which is funded through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! entry.title: Crews work to contain fire in Caldwell
entry.summary: GARDEN CITY — The Idaho Department of Environmental Quality … MERIDIAN — George Seybold, CEO of Meridian’s Talloo marketin… A vegetation fire Wednesday in Caldwell was responded to by 80 firefighters from multiple agencies. CALDWELL — A vegetation fire in northwest Caldwell prompted the actions of dozens of fire fighters across multiple agencies Wednesday. The blaze burning around Look Lane and Highway 26 at the Idaho Materials and Construction site affected approximately 30 acres, according to a news release from the Caldwell Fire Department, and while initial reports indicated that several structures were threatened, crews stopped the fire’s progress before it caused any structure loss. No injuries to firefighters or civilians have been reported. At the incident’s peak, approximately 80 personnel were assigned to the fire. The fire was nearing containment as of Wednesday night, CFD said, adding that crews were expected to remain on scene throughout the night “working in medium to heavy fuels adjacent to the Boise River.” “Crews will continue extinguishing hotspots, strengthening containment lines, and monitoring for any potential flare-ups,” Caldwell Fire stated. “Personnel will likely patrol the fire area over the coming days until significant moisture or precipitation reduces the risk of rekindles.” Caldwell Fire was aided in its response by fire crews from Nampa, Middleton-Star, Wilder, Parma, Fruitland, Sand Hollow, Boise, New Plymouth, Payette, the Whitney Fire District and Caldwell Rural Fire District. Additional assistance was provided by the Bureau of Land Management, Canyon County Emergency Management and Canyon County Paramedics. The cause of the fire remains under investigation. “The Caldwell Fire Department extends its appreciation to all assisting agencies and personnel whose coordinated efforts helped protect lives and property during this incident,” the release states. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! entry.title: Letting them cook: Owyhee students try their hand at business with recipe website
entry.summary: CALDWELL — A vegetation fire in northwest Caldwell prompted … BOISE — Back on track for railroad enthusiasts, the U.S. Dep… Owyhee High School sophmores Mason Seybold and Thomas Day work on their laptops at Talloo Business Hub in Meridian, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. Owyhee High School sophmores Mason Seybold and Thomas Day pause for a photo while working on their website at Talloo Business Hub in Meridian, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. Owyhee High School sophmores Mason Seybold and Thomas Day work on their laptops at Talloo Business Hub in Meridian, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. MERIDIAN — George Seybold, CEO of Meridian’s Talloo marketing firm, issued a summer challenge to his 15-year-old son Mason and his friend Thomas Day. “You’re going to create a business. You take a maximum of one month. I’m going to guide you and help you figure this thing out,” the elder Seybold said. “These young men are saying, ‘how do we do something that’s better than playing video games over the summer?’” George Seybold, CEO of Meridian’s Talloo marketing firm, checks in on his 15-year-old son Mason and his friend Thomas Day as the duo work on their website, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. The teens, incoming sophomores at Owyhee High School, settled on a recipe website as both enjoy cooking. Mason likes to make chocolate chip cookies, while Thomas says he’s “a pasta guy.” They were motivated to created a repository of recipes that were easily accessible to would-be chefs, without ads or lengthy memoirs from bloggers. “We don’t have any of the crazy pop-ups and crazy stories, so it creates a user-friendly experience for people who want to cook,” Thomas said. “A lot of the stories I see, it’s always some sort of family member that passed it down for generations, but they always make it so extremely detailed that I have to scroll for two minutes just to get to the recipe.” Called Le Sapor — Latin for “flavor” — the website currently contains about 700 recipes, from Korean bean pancakes to chicken noodle soup, with more to come. A screenshot from the recipe website lesapor.com. Instead of conventional advertising, the site utilizes a pay-per-click model in which local food producers such as farmers and produce vendors can pay a monthly subscription fee and sign up to embed certain ingredient words in recipes with purchase links to their websites. “(Producers) can sign up for a business account, and we have this one page that lists the producers that exist and their business,” Mason said. “They can get recognition that way, while people are looking through it, or when somebody clicks on one of the recipe ingredients.” The site is on the verge of linking up its first producers, George Seybold said, about a week after it first went active. “We just wanted to acknowledge a bunch of people that put in effort for someone in a home with food,” Thomas said. Owyhee High School sophmores Mason Seybold and Thomas Day work on their laptops at Talloo Business Hub in Meridian, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. One feature of Le Sapor is “recipe mode,” which users can activate when they don’t want to physically touch their phone or computer due to messy fingers. In recipe mode, screens won’t fall asleep and a chef can swipe up or down with motions in front of the camera. Cooks can access the Le Sapor catalog and submit recipes of their own at lesapor.com.

entry.title: Boise grant seeks to restore passenger trains
entry.summary: Something’s in the Treasure Valley air as the weather is exp… Initial motions were made by Boise City Council regarding th… Visitors walk along a path toward the Boise Depot to celebrate the century-old local icon on Wednesday, April 16, 2025. BOISE — Back on track for railroad enthusiasts, the U.S. Department of Transportation has awarded the city of Boise half-a-million dollars to pursue intercity passenger rail service in the city’s future. The grant will allow the city to develop a financing strategy for future passenger rail opportunities, a Wednesday city press release states. For Boise, it will allow the city to look at the Boise Depot and “other station-area investments.” The $503,125 grant comes from the department’s Innovative Financing and Asset Concessions Program which helps communities explore creative approaches to transportation, according to the city. Attempts have been made in recent years by local leaders to revive a line from the Boise Depot to Portland and Salt Lake. However, a mistake in 2023 cost the Idaho Transportation Department a $500,000 grant that would have funded the study, as reported by the Idaho Capital Sun. This new grant will provide a similar opportunity. “Boiseans have made clear they want to bring passenger rail back to get around the Treasure Valley and across the West,” Mayor Lauren McLean said in the release. “This grant helps bring us one step closer to doing just that.” Haadiya Tariq is the Boise reporter for the Idaho Press. entry.title: 12-year-old boy dies after incident at Idaho hot springs
entry.summary: BOISE — Back on track for railroad enthusiasts, the U.S. Dep… BOISE — The impending expansion of the Boise Centre has cont… FEATHERVILLE — A Treasure Valley family is grieving following the death of a boy over the weekend at an Idaho hot springs. Everett Newby, a 12-year-old Boise boy, died Saturday, June 20, at St. Luke’s in Boise after he was “submerged in water for a period of time” at Trinity Hot Springs in Featherville, the Ada County Coroner’s Office announced Wednesday. Newby’s cause and manner of death are listed as pending. The hot springs are located 100 miles east of Boise in Elmore County. An online fundraiser for the boy’s family states that Newby was taken to the hospital via life flight, and he died in the ICU, the coroner’s office stated. As of Wednesday evening, the fundraiser had raised nearly $4,000. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. entry.title: Boise Centre growth continues with third land acquisition
entry.summary: FEATHERVILLE — A Treasure Valley family is grieving followin… MERIDIAN — Former Idaho state Rep. Dorothy Moon was elected … The entrance to the Boise Centre is pictured in this file photo. A real estate hospitality firm has recommended the Greater Boise Auditorium District either expand the center or erect a new multi-purpose indoor sports facility, among other options, as event space in Boise continues to grow. A commercial building stands at the northeast corner of 11th and River streets in Boise, Tuesday, April 7, 2026. The Greater Boise Auditorium District purchased the 4-acre property — plus an additional 1.8-acre parcel — to serve as a stepping stone for the expansion of the Boise Centre. BOISE — The impending expansion of the Boise Centre has continued to grow in size with the purchase of yet another downtown plot. The purchase was approved on Tuesday by the Greater Boise Auditorium District Board, according to a press release. Since a 2025 study by Johnson Consulting recommended the Boise Centre double its event space, the district has acquired land in the downtown area with a mix of possible venues in mind. “As we look to the future of the district and the needs it can serve in the next 10 years and beyond, this collection of properties expands the opportunities to build something that serves the Boise community across multiple facets and invests in the future of downtown Boise,” Board Chair Kristin Muchow said in the release. The center is landlocked among skyscrapers and other developments in the middle of downtown Boise, leaving few options to expand the existing facility. The work-around is to build somewhere else. The three parcels that have been purchased by the district this year are a few blocks away from the Boise Centre, approximately an eight-minute walk. The construction of a new facility will not impact the current operations of the center. The three parcels acquired for future expansion by the Greater Boise Auditorium District are southwest of the Boise Centre. “A key attribute we are looking for with this future project is avoiding becoming landlocked as we are with our current facility,” district Executive Director Cody Lund said in the release. “Through the purchase agreements with all three parcels, we now have the flexibility to imagine a second property that not only expands our current book of convention business but opens up the possibilities for further community collaboration and engagement.” The first parcel was purchased in early April and is a 4-acre lot near the western corner of the 10th and River streets intersection. The second parcel, a 1.8-acre lot, was acquired at the end of April and is just across the street, east of 10th Street. This third lot is on the corner of 9th and Lee streets and adds approximately half an acre of land. The combined three parcels are a total of nearly 6.5 acres. According to the press release, the third parcel purchase “completes the district’s exploratory committee’s initial discussions and evaluations to secure land.” According to Lund, the district does not currently have plans to purchase any additional land. “The district’s exploratory committee has met with numerous landowners, developers, and civic leaders about land opportunities in and around Boise Centre in the past 12 months,” Lund wrote in an email. “At this time, there are no active plans to move forward on additional land, though the district remains open to opportunities that benefit the future development project as we work through what the site components and design could include.” The Boise Centre was last expanded in 2017 to accommodate larger events and more events simultaneously, according to the district’s website. A decade later, it currently functions at near full capacity. The 2025 study by Johnson Consulting found that the 86,000-square-foot center loses an average of 115 events annually due to a lack of available space. Options previously presented to the Greater Boise Auditorium District Board included the construction of new exhibit halls or a two-phase project to also construct ballrooms and meeting space in tandem. Nothing has been finalized. According to the release, the auditorium district will be seeking proposals for studies on the site and affordability of a development. This includes considerations for lobby spaces, kitchen, back of house storage, loading docks and a parking structure. The studies will evaluate the best layouts to maximize efficiency and financial viability. Results are expected to be finalized by the end of the year. “This is the first step in the site evaluation process to determine how different components of a multi-use event facility could best fit on the 6.4 acres,” the release states. “... Future project development for the district is an investment in growth alongside current facilities.”

entry.title: Dorothy Moon reelected to third term as Idaho GOP chair
entry.summary: BOISE — The impending expansion of the Boise Centre has cont… EAGLE — City council members in Eagle have voted to outlaw f… MERIDIAN — Former Idaho state Rep. Dorothy Moon was elected to her third term as chair of the Idaho Republican Party during last week’s Idaho GOP Convention in Meridian. Moon announced her candidacy for a third term as chair earlier this month after first being elected in 2022 and again in 2024. In this year’s election, Moon received 306 votes, former Idaho state Sen. Steven Thayn received 155 votes and Mark Fuller, the previous Idaho GOP first vice chair, came in third with 134 votes. In receiving just over 51% of the vote, Moon was able to narrowly avoid a runoff. In his challenge to Moon, Thayn had called for fostering a greater sense of unity among members of the party. “Unity does not mean uniformity of opinion,” Thayn said in a prior news release on his candidacy. “Nor does it mean forced agreement. Forced unity always leads to tyranny. Real unity is voluntary. It is built through listening, respect, and a sincere effort to solve problems together.” In the initial announcement of Moon’s campaign, she highlighted improvements to voter turnout and the defeat of the 2024 ranked-choice voting ballot initiative as among key party successes during her most recent term. “It has been an honor to serve Idaho Republicans during a time of tremendous growth and energy within our party,” Moon said in the release. “Together, we have worked to keep Idaho the conservative leader of the nation, defend election integrity, support our county parties and grassroots volunteers, and elect Republicans who stand firmly for the values of faith, family, freedom, and limited government.” The Idaho GOP did not respond to multiple requests seeking comment from Moon on her victory and the party’s future direction. Platform changes adopted last week include direction for the Idaho State Legislature to eliminate property taxes in the state. In the near term, the attention of the party is once again turning to pending ballot initiatives on medical cannabis and abortion that, like ranked-choice voting, are being opposed by the Idaho Republican Party. “Idaho Republicans share a common commitment to protecting our state from the failed policies we see spreading across the country,” Moon said in the release. “Now is the time to continue building a strong, unified organization capable of winning elections, protecting our most vulnerable, and preserving Idaho’s unique way of life for future generations.” Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. entry.title: Citing severe fire threat, Eagle council members ban fireworks in parts of foothills
entry.summary: MERIDIAN — Former Idaho state Rep. Dorothy Moon was elected … BOISE — The U.S. Marshals Service’s Greater Idaho Fugitive T… This map shows the areas designated as the Wildland-Urban Interface Overlay District, where fireworks have been banned. EAGLE — City council members in Eagle have voted to outlaw fireworks in areas of the foothills that present a severe fire threat. The council approved the resolution Tuesday declaring a restriction on the use of fireworks in areas designated as the Wildland-Urban Interface Overlay District (WUI), which encompasses the Eagle foothills north of Beacon Light Road between Highway 16 and Highway 55, as well as the AdaEagle Bike Park, according to a city of Eagle news release. Those areas contain vegetative conditions that constitute a severe wildfire threat during fire season — approximately June 1 through Oct. 31. The release notes that with the recent mild winter and historically low snowpack, the vegetation in the foothills has dried earlier and become a significantly greater risk to wildfire. “We are not trying to take away the fun of blowing-off fireworks, we’re talking about the worst of the worst areas — and it is a severe fire threat,” Council Member Robert Gillis said at the Tuesday meeting. Ada County commissioners last week voted to ban fireworks in unincorporated areas of the county, some of which overlap with the Eagle WUI. “If you see smoke, make a phone call. Don’t assume it’s a controlled burn — if you see something, say something,” Mayor Brad Pike said during the meeting. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press.
